clean_story: Spooky action at distance.  Particles that "know" each other. "Quantum ghosts" isn’t a scientific term—it’s a way to picture quantum entanglement, where two particles stay mysteriously linked, no matter how far apart. Imagine two coins flipped in perfect sync—one lands heads in New York, the other instantly tails in Tokyo. No signal travels between them. They just are connected, like twins feeling each other’s pain across oceans. Here’s the weird part:Measure one particle’s spin, and its partner immediately matches—even if light would take years to cross the gap. Einstein called this "spooky," thinking it proved quantum theory was incomplete. Experiments now confirm it’s real. In 2017, Chinese scientists entangled photons on a satellite 1,200 km apart. No wires. No delay. Just ghostly coordination. This isn’t telepathy—it’s deeper. The particles aren’t sending messages; they’re parts of one invisible whole, like two ends of the same string. Three related subjects are hidden connections, faster-than-light limits and quantum networks.
